summary: McKenzie describes the origin story of his first micro-ISV (uISV), which grew from noticing an unmet need in the ESL teaching community in Japan. A teacher's email requesting software to automate a tedious classroom task revealed a genuine market gap — no good solutions existed despite the problem being straightforward to solve programmatically. After building a rough prototype in six hours that generated overwhelming demand from a small mailing list, he later decided to rebuild it properly as a commercial product, reaching 70% completion in just four days. The post emphasizes targeting niche markets where major developers won't bother competing.
key_points: Real business ideas come from observing unmet needs in communities you already belong to, not from abstract brainstorming
A prototype built in six hours generated overwhelming demand from a 60-person mailing list, validating the market before any commercial investment
Targeting niche markets is the key strategic advantage for solo developers — problems too small for big companies are perfect for uISVs
99% of the work in simple applications is user interface design, not business logic
Strong design documents written before coding are essential — skipping UI mockups led to spaghetti code in the View layer
money_quotes: One tip: 99% of the work in designing this sort of simple application is user interface.
The main tip I can give is to target a niche market.

There aren't 5 million people in the US who will ever do X in their lives, so its so below the radar screen of major software developers and even uISVs who haven't been teachers themselves that nobody has ever decided to spend ~30 developer hours and do a proper job of X.

I mailed the teacher the program and, on a lark, responded to the mailing list that I had something which would do X and to get a hold of me if you needed it. I had 3 messages by the time I left work.
The next day I showed up to work, opened my inbox, and the folder which handles replies to that mailing list was stuffed.
Please please please let me have it! was the general tone.
Strong design documents, don't leave home without them.
key_insight: The best micro-ISV opportunities hide in niche communities where a straightforward technical solution to a tedious manual task can generate passionate demand precisely because the market is too small for anyone else to bother serving.
